title: Breitmannן¿½s Last Ballads - The Magic Shoes
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It was stiller, dimmer twilight
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ amber toorninן¿½ into gold,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Like young maidensן¿½ hairs get yellow
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ und more dark as dey crow old;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und dere shtood a high ruine
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ vhere de Donau rooshed along,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All lofely, yet neclected
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ like an oldt und silent song.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Out shpoke der Ritter Breitmann,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Ven I hafe not forgot,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ich kenn an anciendt shtory
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ of dis inderesdin shpot,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of the Deutscher Middleolter
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ vot de Minnesingers sung,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ven dot olt ruine oben
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ vas a-bloomin, fair, und yung.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Vonce dere lifed a noble frן¿½ulein
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ fery peautiful vas she,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ More ash twendy dimes goot lookin
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ it is in de historie;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und mit more ash forty quarters
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ on her woppenshield, dot men
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Might beholdt mitout a discount
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ she vas of de upper ten.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But dough lofely as an angel,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ mit eyes of turkos plue,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She vas cruel ash a teufel,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ und de vorst man efer knew.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vonce ven a nople young one
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ kneeled down to her mit lofe,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She kicket him mit her slipper
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ und oopset him on de shtove.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Und said, ן¿½I do refuse you,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ as you may plainly see;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und from dis day henseforvart
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ mine refuse you shall pe,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und when I do run afder you
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ like dogs run afder men,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Den I vill pe your vife, yung man
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ boot keep avay dill denn!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½He lishten to her crimly,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ and no single vort he said,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Boot de bitter dings she spoken
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ poot der teufel in his head;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For she hafe not learned de visdom,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ vich is alvays safe and sound,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Donן¿½t go to pourinן¿½ water on a mouse
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ven id ist trowned.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Vonce, at de end of autoom,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ven de vind vos bitter cold,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dis maiden out a-ridinן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ met a voman poor and old;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Her feets vere bare and pleedinן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ and she said, ן¿½Ah! tonן¿½t refuse
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To gife me, nople lady,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ yoosht de vorst of your oldt shoes!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½De lady boorst out laughinן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Fool here, or fool me dere,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You give to me a couple,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I gives to you a pair.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Denn she rode avay a-laughinן¿½;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ de old voman says ן¿½I wete,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½ll give you shoes, my lady,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ dot vill fit your soul and feet!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Dis voman vas a vitchן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ an bitter one dere to,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All dot vot she had shpoken
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ she light enough could do;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ De Ritter did not know it,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ but he told her of his love,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And how dot shkornful lady
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ hat oopset him mit de shtove.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Out spoke de grimme witchן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½She shall pay dee well to boot,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If yo pring to me de measure
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ of dat ladyן¿½s liddle foot.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He got it from her shoemaker,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ and gafe id to de vitch,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Denn she gafe it to de damsel
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ pooty soon as hot as pitch.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Von morn de lofely lady,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ on openinן¿½ her toor,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Found de nicest pair of gaiter boots
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ she efer saw pefore;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dey vitted her exoctly
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ mitouten any doubt
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Boot, mein Gott! how she vas shrocken
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ven dey ן¿½gun to valk apout!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Und ash de poots go valkinן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ like de buds go mit de stem,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It vollowed dot de lady
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ had to valk apout in dem.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dey took her out into de street
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ dey run her on de road,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bym-by she saw a man ahead
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ vot led her vhere she goed.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Vhen he vent valkinן¿½ longsome
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ denn longsome vas her pace,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vhen he roon like a greyhound
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ she skompered in a race;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He led her oן¿½er de moundains
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ und cross de lonely plain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Until de eveninן¿½ shadows,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ven he took her home again.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Denn she dink mit hate and fury
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ of dis man she used to skoff,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und den go at de gaiters
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ boot she couldnן¿½t pull dem off,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She vork mit all de servants,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ boot ן¿½tvasent any use,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und so she hafe to go to bett
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ a-shleepinן¿½ in her shoes.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Next morninן¿½ off dey shtarted,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ apout de broke of day,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Den he led her to a castle
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ in de woods and far away,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And shpeak to her, ן¿½My lady
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I dink at last you see
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dat de dime has come in earnesdt
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ vhen youן¿½ve cot to vollow me!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Oh vat ish female nature?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh vat ish mortal pride?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How all dot shtands de firmest
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ most quickly shlips aside
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ De cloudts dot oן¿½er de moundains
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ look shkornful at de plain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ere long mit shtormy wetter
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ come toomble down in rain.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½So de storm-cloud of Superbia
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ vhich shweep her soul above,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vas meltet mit his shternness
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ and be-turn?d into love,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As his words like donner wetter
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ croshed ven de lightninן¿½ flies,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So downward coom de torrents
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ of dear trops from her eyes.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Und she gry, ן¿½Mit shame I own it,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ to say de fery least,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I gonfess dat in dis matter
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I hafe acted like a peast;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ven I made of you my refuse,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I dinked it no account,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But now de pack is on my back
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ it seems a big amount.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½But if you vish to ved me,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I vill do vat you require.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He answered, ן¿½Now youן¿½re talkinן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ dot is yoost vot I tesire,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For I am very willinן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ and you do not refuse,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Boot remember vot you bromised
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ send de vitch a pair of shoes!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½She answered, ן¿½I vill follow
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ verever you may go,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All ofer hills and falleys,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ in sunshine, rain, or schnow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All over in der Welt, dear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½ll vander on vith thee,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I do not care how rough de road
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ or dark de path may be!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½Or in de bloominן¿½ meadows,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ vhere de grass is soft and sweet,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or in de rocky passes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ vhere de stones are under veet,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or if I vear de shoes, love,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ vitch you hafe given me,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or if I moost go barefoot,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ is all de same to me.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½He drew away de gaiters.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She said, ן¿½As Iן¿½m rich
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I vill fill dem both mit money,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ and take dem to de vitch.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ja wohl, she saw die Hexe,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ and takinן¿½ her aside,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She danked her for de lesson
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ vot hat dook avay her pride.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½On de vay vhen dey vere married,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ how vere dey all erstaun
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To see a lofely lady
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ come in mit golden crown,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All in a rosy-silken dress
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ vot shined as pright as glass,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Said, ן¿½My dears, I am de vitch
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ dot fetch dis ding to pass.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½You know I look so ogly vonce,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ und now am peautiful,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dot ist de vay dot all dings vork
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ven folks pe dutiful.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ash de lily toorns to vhitey
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ vot once vas dirty green,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So all ist fair ven virdue
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ist runninן¿½ de machine.ן¿½ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dis is de vondrous shtory
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ vot de Ritter Breitmann told
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Besides the rooshinן¿½ Danube
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ of de schloss so grey und old,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vhile a shmokinן¿½ of his meerschaum;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ und till all time pe gone
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The rustlinן¿½ of de vasser
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ tells de tale for ever on.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dat is an alt legende,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ und yet ן¿½Tis efer new,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und to efery von dot hears it
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ it fits yoost like a shoe.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und dis de shininן¿½ moral
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ dot in de oyster lies
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Some day you may roon after
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ de dings you vonce despise!
meaning:
This evocative piece by Charles G. Leland represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Charles G. Leland's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
